title_screen: ^You're about to experience an internship from start to finish — six stages, from scanning the job board to your final exit interview.
^Every choice you make shapes what happens next. Some paths only open if you've built enough confidence or made the right connections along the way.
^Before we begin, choose how you'd like the story told:

b: ^Great choice. Think of me as a friend who's been through this before — someone in your corner. I'll guide you through each stage, and we'll figure it out together. Ready?
^Understood. This simulation will walk you through each stage of an internship placement in a realistic workplace context. Your decisions will have professional consequences.
^MODE SELECTED: Playful. Achievement unlocked: "Ready Player Intern" 🎮. Your Readiness Score starts at zero. Every choice earns — or costs — you points. Let's see what you're made of.
^You walk up to the WorkReady Jobs board and take a deep breath. There's a lot here — six different organisations, each offering something different. Don't rush. Have a look around.
^The WorkReady Jobs board displays current internship vacancies across six Perth-based organisations. Each listing includes the company name, sector, and available role.
^🎯 STAGE 1: THE JOB BOARD. Six companies. Six roles. One you. Scan the board and pick your quest!
^ — that could be a really great fit. But here's the thing...

board_choice: ^You check the deadline. Applications close in two hours.
^Your resume isn't tailored to this role. You have a generic version saved, but it doesn't mention anything specific to

b: ^Two hours isn't much. What do you want to do?
^You must decide how to proceed within the time constraint.
^⏰ THE CLOCK IS TICKING! What's your move?

board_choice: ^Rush it — submit my generic resume now
^Wait — I'll miss this one but prepare properly for the next round

b: ^Okay — sometimes you've got to go for it. You hit submit with your heart pounding. It's done. But that nagging feeling that you could have done more? That's worth remembering.
^You submit the generic resume. The application is lodged within the deadline. However, the resume does not address the specific requirements of the role.
^SPEED RUN! 💨 Resume submitted. But it's... generic. The recruiter might notice. Confidence -5.
^Smart call. It's hard to let one go, but you'll be in a much stronger position next time. The
^ comes around again in the next intake, and this time you'll be ready.
^You allow the deadline to pass. The same role is listed again in the next intake cycle, giving you time to prepare a targeted application.
^PATIENCE UNLOCKED! 🧘 You let this one go. The same role reappears next intake. Confidence +5. Smart play.
^Before we move on — let's take a quick peek at what would have happened if you'd chosen the other path.
^If you'd waited, you'd have missed this specific role — but you'd have had time to research
^ properly and tailor a stronger application for the next intake. Sometimes patience pays.
^Had you waited, this listing would have closed without your application. However, the role reopens next intake, allowing for a more competitive submission with proper preparation.
^🔮 If you'd WAITED: missed this quest, but next intake = stronger app + Confidence +5. Patience meta unlocks late game.
^If you'd rushed it, you'd have submitted a generic resume. The recruiter would have flagged it as untailored, and your confidence would have taken a knock for ignoring that nagging feeling.
^Had you rushed, you would have submitted a generic application unlikely to clear initial screening, with a corresponding hit to confidence.
^🔮 If you'd RUSHED: generic resume, recruiter side-eye, Confidence -5. Speed kills.
^Alright — Stage 2. This is where you put yourself on paper. A good resume doesn't just list what you've done. It tells the reader why you're right for this role.
^Stage 2: Resume Submission. You are preparing your application for the
^. The quality of your resume will determine whether you are invited to interview.
^📝 STAGE 2: THE RESUME. Time to craft your character sheet! What do you lead with?
^Your generic resume is already submitted, but
^ allows applicants to update their materials before the review date. You have a second chance to improve it.
^You have time to build a strong, tailored resume from scratch. What do you want to lead with?

submitting_the_resume: ^Lead with skills relevant to the role

b: ^Solid foundation! Your grades show you can do the work. Though on its own, every applicant leads with grades — you might not stand out just yet.
^Academic credentials are included. This demonstrates baseline competence but does not differentiate you from other applicants.
^📊 GPA loaded! It's a safe play — reliable but not flashy.
^That's the way. You've looked at what
^ actually needs and matched your skills to it. That shows initiative — and recruiters notice.
^Role-relevant skills are highlighted. This directly addresses the selection criteria and demonstrates you have researched the position.
^🎯 Direct hit! You matched your skills to the job ad. Recruiters love that.
^There's something nice about showing who you are beyond the textbook. It makes you memorable. Just make sure the connection to the role is clear.
^Personal interests are included. While this adds personality to the application, it may not directly address the role requirements.
^🎸 Going for the personality angle! Bold choice. Confidence +5 for being yourself.
^Good start. Now — do you want to add anything else before you submit?

^Round 2! Add another layer to your resume:

resume_second_choice: ^Add a short portfolio of relevant work

b: ^A tailored cover letter can make all the difference. You take the time to explain why
^ matters to you — not just what you can do, but why you want to do it here.

^ is your top pick. Major bonus.

^Now that's impressive. You've put together a small portfolio that shows — not just tells — what you can do. The kind of thing that makes a recruiter pause and look twice.
^A portfolio of relevant work samples is attached. This provides concrete evidence of capability and is a strong differentiator.
^💼 PORTFOLIO UNLOCKED! (Required Confidence ≥ 55) You showed your work. Massive advantage.
^Sometimes done is better than perfect. You hit submit and move on.
^No additional materials are added. The application is submitted in its current state.
^YOLO SUBMIT! 🚀 No extras. Let's see if it sticks.
^Your application is strong. Really strong. You've shown
^ exactly who you are and why you belong there. Well done — that took effort, and it shows.
^Your application is comprehensive and well-targeted. Based on the quality of your materials, you are highly likely to receive an interview invitation.
^Solid work. Your resume might not blow anyone away, but it's honest and shows you've thought about the role. That puts you ahead of plenty of applicants.
^Your application meets the minimum standard for shortlisting. An interview invitation is probable but not certain.
^The resume is... fine. But fine might not be enough in a competitive field. Don't worry — there's always something to learn from this.
^Your application is below the expected standard for shortlisting. The resume lacks specificity and does not address the role requirements directly.
^/6). This might be a tough sell...
^Whatever you submitted, it's out of your hands now. The waiting begins.
^The application is now under review. The next stage commences upon a response from
^📤 RESUME SUBMITTED. Now we wait. Loading next stage...
^Stage 3. The interview. Deep breath — this is the part most people dread. But here's a secret: preparation matters more than perfection.
^ has reviewed your application and will now determine whether to proceed with an interview.
^🎤 STAGE 3: THE INTERVIEW. Boss battle incoming! (Or not, depending on that resume...)
^A few days pass... and then the email arrives. "Thank you for your interest, but we've decided to progress other candidates." It stings. But it's not the end.
^You receive a standard rejection email. Your application did not meet the shortlisting threshold. This outcome reflects the quality of your submitted materials.
^📧 REJECTED. Your resume didn't make the cut. Game over? Not quite...
^Here's the thing about rejection — it teaches you something if you let it. You take a moment to look at your resume again, and this time you see the gaps. That awareness? That's growth.
^You review your application materials and identify areas for improvement. This reflective process, while uncomfortable, builds self-awareness that will strengthen future applications.
^🔄 REFLECTION BONUS! You reviewed what went wrong. Experience gained!
^But the simulation continues — imagine you'd applied more carefully, and
^ called you in. What would you do next?
^For learning purposes, the simulation will now proceed as though you received an interview invitation.
^SECOND CHANCE ACTIVATED! Let's pretend that resume was better. The interview awaits!
^Good news — your phone buzzes with an email from
^. They want to meet you! The interview is scheduled for Thursday at 10 AM.
^. The interview is scheduled for Thursday at 10:00 AM at their Perth CBD office.

^It's Wednesday evening. The interview is tomorrow morning. You'd planned to prepare tonight, but a friend called with a crisis and you spent the evening helping them. Now it's 11 PM and you haven't done any interview prep. You feel underprepared.
^The evening before the interview, an unexpected personal commitment prevents your planned preparation. You are now underprepared with limited time remaining.
^⚡ PLOT TWIST! Your prep time vanished (friend emergency). It's 11 PM. Interview is at 10 AM. You are NOT ready.
^Brave call. Maybe a little reckless, but sometimes you've just got to show up and be yourself. Let's see how it goes.
^You decide to attend without additional preparation. This carries risk but demonstrates commitment.
^🎲 WINGING IT! Bold move. Let's roll the dice...
^That takes courage too — admitting you're not ready and asking for more time.
^'s HR responds the next morning: they can fit you in next Tuesday. You spend the weekend preparing properly.
^ to request a rescheduling. HR accommodates with a new slot the following week. You use the additional time for structured preparation.
^📅 RESCHEDULE REQUESTED! They moved it to next Tuesday. You spent the weekend cramming. Readiness +1!
^You're sitting in the interview room. The hiring manager smiles, makes some small talk, and then asks their first real question:
^The interview commences. After introductions, the hiring manager poses the following question:
^🎬 INTERVIEW START! The hiring manager leans forward and asks:
^"Tell me about a time you had to solve a problem under pressure. What did you do?"
^<i>"What specifically interests you about working in this sector? Why
^"How do you handle feedback that you disagree with?"

interview_questions: ^Be honest — share a real experience, even if imperfect
^Freeze up — stumble through an unclear response

b: ^Your answer is... fine. Safe. The hiring manager nods politely but doesn't lean in. You can tell they've heard this kind of answer before.
^Your response is competent but generic. It does not differentiate you from other candidates. The interviewer notes your answer without follow-up questions.
^📝 Safe answer delivered. The interviewer nods. It's... fine. Just fine.

^You take a breath and tell them something real. It's not a polished story — you stumbled, you learned, you grew. The hiring manager's expression changes. They lean in. "That's interesting — tell me more."
^You provide an authentic, specific example. While the outcome wasn't perfect, your self-awareness and learning orientation are evident. The interviewer engages with follow-up questions.
^💡 AUTHENTICITY BONUS! The interviewer actually leaned in. They asked follow-up questions. You stood out!

^The words don't come. You start a sentence, stop, start again. The hiring manager waits patiently, but you can feel the moment slipping. It's okay — this happens to everyone. What matters is what you do next.
^You are unable to articulate a clear response. The interviewer allows a pause but moves on to the next question. This has negatively impacted your presentation.
^😰 FREEZE! Words.exe has stopped responding. Confidence -10. Ouch.
^Because you rescheduled and prepared, you handle the rest of the interview with much more composure. The extra time made a real difference.
^Your rescheduling decision proves effective. The additional preparation time results in a more confident and structured interview performance.
^📈 PREP BONUS ACTIVE! Rescheduling paid off — you handled the rest with extra composure.
^The interview wraps up. Whatever happened in there, you showed up. That counts. The offer comes through a few days later —
^ wants you to start next Monday.
^ extends an offer for the internship position. Your start date is the following Monday.
^ sends an offer! You're in! Starting Monday!

interview_outcome: ^What if I'd handled the prep differently?

b: ^A glimpse at what the alternative might have looked like:

^If you'd gone in unprepared, you'd have walked in with the interview question still rattling around your head. You might have stumbled — confidence taking a hit — but you'd also have learned something about yourself under pressure.

^Had you proceeded without preparation, your performance would have been inconsistent and your confidence likely diminished. However, the experience of interviewing under-prepared has its own learning value.
^🔮 If you'd WINGED IT: probable freeze, Confidence -10, but XP for surviving. Live to fight another day.

^If you'd asked to reschedule, the hiring manager would likely have respected the honesty. You'd have lost a small amount of confidence in the moment, but gained a weekend of proper prep — and probably a much smoother interview the second time around.
^Had you requested rescheduling, the additional preparation time would have improved your interview performance and reflected self-awareness — typically valued by hiring managers.
^🔮 If you'd RESCHEDULED: short-term Confidence -5, long-term Confidence +10, prep bonus active. Knowing when to pause = power.
^Stage 4. You've made it — you're actually here, at
^, sitting at a real desk with a real login and a real manager who just handed you your first real task.
^Stage 4: The Work Task. Your first week at
^, has assigned your first substantive task.
^💼 STAGE 4: THE WORK TASK. First week on the job! Your manager
^ has a task for you. Let's see what you've got.

^ seems busy — they're always in meetings or rushing between conversations. But they took the time to explain the task clearly before heading off to their next call: pull together a summary of recent project data and flag anything that looks unusual.
^ briefs you on the task: compile and summarise recent project data, flagging any anomalies. The deliverable is due by end of day Thursday.
^ is visibly occupied with other commitments.
^THE TASK: Compile project data, find anomalies, deliver summary by Thursday.
^ drops the brief and vanishes into meetings. You're on your own... or are you?

completing_the_work_task: ^You're halfway through when you hit a wall. The data format doesn't match what
^ described. You can't tell if it's an error in the data or if you're misunderstanding the brief.
^ is in a meeting behind a closed door. A colleague, Sam, is working at the desk across from you.

b: ^What do you do? There's no wrong answer here — but each path teaches you something different.
^Select your approach to resolving this blocker.

completing_the_work_task: ^Ask Sam, the colleague across from you

b: ^You wait. And wait. The meeting runs over by 45 minutes. When

^ trusts you a little more for not panicking.

^, they clarify the data format and note your professionalism in waiting.
^ clarifies the issue but you're now behind schedule. Manager relationship +1 though!

^'s meeting. The issue is resolved quickly and the task is completed on time. However,

^ answers (slightly annoyed). Problem solved fast, task done on time! But Manager relationship -1. Worth it?

^Sam looks up, smiles. "Oh yeah, that data format changed last month — here, let me show you." In five minutes, you're back on track. And you've just made your first workplace connection. That matters more than you might think right now.

^Sam is familiar with the data format change and explains it immediately. The issue is resolved without delay. You complete the task on time and have established a productive working relationship with a colleague.
^🤝 SAM TO THE RESCUE! Problem solved in 5 minutes. Colleague relationship +2! And you unlocked the "Asks For Help" trait!

^Stage 5. Not every important moment at work happens at your desk. Sometimes the real test is in the lunchroom.
^Stage 5: The Lunchroom Moment. Workplace social dynamics often play out in informal settings. This stage tests your ability to navigate an unscripted social situation.
^🍰 STAGE 5: THE LUNCHROOM MOMENT. SOCIAL ENCOUNTER! This isn't about spreadsheets — it's about people.
^ You head into the breakout space — standing desks pushed back, kombucha taps to one side —
^ You duck into the smoko shed behind the main office, the kettle hissing on the bench —
^ You walk into the polished kitchen on the consultant floor, espresso machine humming —
^ You head into the staff tea room, where the biscuit tin and the same mugs have probably been there since the eighties —
^ You step into the glass-walled meeting room they've repurposed for the occasion —
^ You walk into the shared community space, the one with the mismatched mugs and the wonky kettle —

the_lunchroom_moment: ^and find a dozen colleagues gathered around a cake. It's someone's birthday — a team leader named Jordan you haven't met yet.

b: ^Someone starts singing Happy Birthday, and the whole group joins in. A few people glance at you standing near the doorway. You don't know the words to the second verse — does anyone? — but the group is warm and the energy is infectious.
^The group begins singing Happy Birthday. You are standing at the periphery. Several colleagues make eye contact with you. Participation expectations in this social context are ambiguous.
^🎵 HAPPY BIRTHDAY TO YOU... The singing starts. Everyone's looking at the cake. A few people glance your way. SOCIAL AWARENESS CHECK:

the_lunchroom_moment: ^Join in — sing along and grab a slice of cake
^Smile and wave from the side — don't sing, but stay present

b: ^You jump in. Your singing is terrible — truly awful — but nobody cares. Jordan catches your eye and grins. "Thanks for joining in! I don't think we've met — you must be the new intern." Just like that, you're part of the group.
^You participate in the celebration. Jordan introduces themselves and welcomes you. Several colleagues engage you in brief conversation. You have successfully integrated into a social setting.
^🎶 YOU SANG! (Badly, but enthusiastically.) Jordan introduces themselves. You're officially part of the crew! Colleagues +2, Confidence +5!
^You hang back with a smile, clapping when the song ends. A colleague next to you leans over — "Smart move, I can't sing either." You share a laugh. Sometimes being present is enough.
^You observe from the periphery while maintaining engaged body language. A colleague acknowledges your presence. You demonstrate social awareness without overstepping as a new team member.
^👋 STRATEGIC POSITIONING! You stayed, you smiled, you clapped. Social Awareness +2! Sometimes the best move is the subtle one.
^You slip out. In the corridor, you wonder if anyone noticed. They probably did. It's not a disaster — but you missed a chance to connect, and those chances don't come around as often as you'd think.

^You exit the lunchroom. While this avoids an uncomfortable situation, it represents a missed opportunity for informal networking. Social withdrawal in workplace settings can be perceived as disengagement.
^🚶 RETREAT! You left the party. Nobody said anything but... you missed a connection opportunity. Confidence -5. Social Awareness -1.
^Small moments like these add up. The people you connect with — or don't — shape what your internship feels like day to day. It's not just about the work.

^Workplace social dynamics contribute significantly to internship outcomes. Relationship building in informal contexts affects collaboration, mentorship opportunities, and overall workplace satisfaction.
^🏢 Social encounters matter more than you think. Your workplace rep is building... or isn't.
^A glimpse at the other ways that moment could have played out:
^If you'd hung back with a smile, you'd still have made a connection — quieter, more observational. Sometimes that's the right read of the room.
^Had you observed without participating, you would have demonstrated social awareness without the risk of overstepping.
^🔮 If you'd WAVED: Social Awareness +2. Subtle but sound.
^If you'd left, you'd have missed the moment entirely — and Jordan would have remembered the new face that didn't stay.
^Had you left, you would have avoided discomfort but lost an informal networking opportunity. Social withdrawal is often noticed.
^🔮 If you'd LEFT: Confidence -5, Social Awareness -1. Missed XP.
^If you'd jumped in and sung, you'd have made a louder splash — Jordan would have introduced themselves directly, and you'd have walked out of there feeling lighter.
^Had you actively participated, you would have built stronger immediate connections at the cost of slightly less observational distance.
^🔮 If you'd SUNG: Colleagues +2, Confidence +5. Bigger splash.
^If you'd left, you'd have ducked the moment entirely — but you'd also have ducked the chance to be seen as part of the team.
^Had you left, you would have avoided the discomfort but missed a networking opportunity that costs little to participate in.
^🔮 If you'd LEFT: missed connection, Social Awareness -1. Avoidance ≠ awareness.
^If you'd joined in singing, you'd have laughed at your own bad voice and walked out with Jordan knowing your name. The team would have remembered the new intern who showed up.
^Had you participated, you would have built relationships and social standing in the team — typically a high-value, low-cost workplace move.
^🔮 If you'd SUNG: Colleagues +2, Confidence +5. The big move.
^Even if you'd just smiled and waved from the side, you'd have stayed present — and that quiet participation often counts more than people realise.
^Had you remained while observing, you would have demonstrated social awareness without overstepping. A safer, still-positive option.
^🔮 If you'd WAVED: Social Awareness +2. The middle path was right there.
^Stage 6. The final chapter. Twelve weeks have flown by, and now you're sitting across from
^ one last time. This isn't an assessment — it's a conversation. A chance to reflect on what happened and what you'll take with you.
^Stage 6: The Exit Interview. Your 12-week internship at
^, your line manager, conducts the exit interview. This is a reflective and feedback-oriented conversation.
^🏁 STAGE 6: THE EXIT INTERVIEW. Final stage! Twelve weeks done. Time to reflect, receive feedback, and close the loop.

the_exit_interview: ^ starts with an open question: "What do you think went well during your time here?"
^The work — I improved at the technical tasks
^The people — I learned how to work in a team
^Honestly, I'm not sure — it was overwhelming

b: ^You talk about how the work challenged you — how that first data task felt impossible, and how by week eight you were handling similar tasks without breaking a sweat.
^You identify specific technical skills developed during the placement.
^ acknowledges your competency development and notes it in the exit documentation.
^ about Sam's help in that first week, about the birthday singing, about learning to read the room in meetings.
^ smiles. "That's what most people miss. The work skills come with time — the people skills are what make you someone others want to work with."
^You emphasise interpersonal and collaborative skills developed through workplace relationships.
^ notes that these "soft skills" are frequently cited as the most valuable internship outcome by employers.
^ says that's what separates good interns from great ones. Readiness +2!
^ leans back. "That's actually one of the most self-aware things an intern has said to me. The fact that you recognise it was overwhelming means you were paying attention. That's not nothing."
^You express that the experience was challenging to process.

^ respects the vulnerability. "Self-awareness is a skill too." Confidence +5!
^ shifts gears. "I want to give you some honest feedback too. Is that okay?"
^ transitions to constructive feedback. "I'd like to share some observations from the team."
^ says: "You kept to yourself quite a bit. The team noticed. I'd encourage you to be more proactive about building connections in your next role — it makes the work better for everyone."
^ adds: "And one more thing — there were times I wasn't sure where you were at with tasks. A quick check-in goes a long way."
^ says: "There were times I wasn't sure where you were at with tasks. Next time, try checking in more regularly with your manager — even a quick update goes a long way."
^ adds: "And — I noticed you tended to work through problems alone. Asking for help isn't a weakness."
^ says: "I noticed you tended to work through problems on your own, even when you were stuck. Asking for help isn't a weakness — it's a skill. Don't be afraid to use it."
^ adds: "And — workplace culture takes time to read. Pay attention to the social dynamics; they matter more than you think."
^ says: "Workplace culture can be tricky to navigate. I'd encourage you to pay more attention to the social dynamics around you — joining in, reading the room. It matters more than you think."
^ says: "Honestly, you did well across the board. My only note is to keep challenging yourself — don't get comfortable. Push for the harder tasks next time."
